cataloging_subject.label: Legends of the Holy Rood: Symbols of the Passion and Cross-Poems
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloging subject is the edition-defined selection represented by University of Michigan item AHA2702.0001.001: thirteen medieval items or witness-versions from Richard Morris's numbered texts II–IV and VI–XIII, plus three appendix works, as encoded from printed pp. 17–97 and 107–224 of Morris's 1871 EETS volume. It is not one homogeneous medieval work, all versions of any constituent work, Morris's complete volume, the omitted Old English items I and V, the omitted front or back matter, the whole manuscripts or 1493 book from which the texts were edited, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML.

catalog_summary.region: England (multiple textual and dialect regions); edited and published in London

date_statements.display: Represented textual states are attested from about 1300 or the early fourteenth century through the 1493 Golden Legend printing
notes: This range dates witnesses and the source print, not composition of all constituent works. Morris dated Ashmole 43 to the late thirteenth century, while the current Bodleian catalog dates it to the first quarter of the fourteenth.

form_statements.description: The selection combines thirteen verse-only items, two prose-only Golden Legend items, and one mixed verse/prose Horae item.
Verse prayers with prose or rubric-like material and Latin incipits.

summaries.value: An edition-defined anthology of Middle English traditions concerning the Holy Cross and Christ's Passion. It juxtaposes alternate manuscript versions of True Cross legends, poems on finding and exalting the Cross, two versions of a debate between Mary and the Cross, short Passion refrains, prose excerpts from the 1493 English Golden Legend, two text-image versions of the Symbols of the Passion, and three appendix works on Mary and the Cross, church festivals, and the Hours of the Cross.

The encoded selection preserves thirteen main-text items or witness-versions—Morris II twice, III, IV, VI, VII, VIII, IX, X, XI, XII, and XIII twice—and three appendix works. Front matter, Old English item I, Old English item V, and back matter are represented only by omitted-content scaffolding and are outside the cataloged subject.

Version labels are witness- and edition-specific. The two item-II texts, the two item-XIII texts, and the two Mary-and-the-Cross texts are not collapsed into single abstract works; Morris's direct sources, collation sources, digital surrogates, and the encoded selection remain separate in the lineage manifest.

extent.description: The cataloged hierarchy has two editorial containers and sixteen represented intellectual items or witness-versions: thirteen in the main text and three in the appendix. The main text contributes 4,067 L elements in 129 LG containers and the appendix 740 L elements in 66 LG containers, for 4,807 L and 195 LG overall. Form classification counts thirteen verse-only items, two prose-only Golden Legend items, and one mixed Horae item. Sixty-eight represented P elements comprise ten Golden Legend prose paragraphs, fifty Symbols illustration/text placeholders, and eight Horae paragraphs or rubric-like blocks; P is not an item count. Five further empty P placeholders belong to omitted item V, and three P elements outside the represented divisions belong to carrier/header/title structures, accounting for the XML-wide total of 76 P. The XML also has 272 PB and 151 MILESTONE elements, but these are carrier and source-locus scaffolding rather than additional intellectual items. Printed-page coverage is discontinuous: pp. 17–97 and 107–224.

content_parts.label: Main-text selection: Morris II–IV and VI–XIII
content_parts.summary: Container for thirteen represented items or witness-versions, excluding Old English items I and V; child work parts carry the additive item and line metrics.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[4], excluding DIV2[@TYPE='omitted Old English legend']; printed pp. 17–97 and 107–193

content_parts.label: II. Þe Holy Rode — Ashmole version
content_parts.summary: A South English Legendary Holy Rood version edited from Ashmole 43 and collated with the imperfect Harley 2277 text.

content_parts.label: II. Hou þe Holy Cros Was Y-founde — Vernon version
content_parts.summary: The Vernon Manuscript version of the same broad Holy Rood tradition, printed in parallel with the Ashmole version.

content_parts.label: III. The Story of the Holy Rood
content_parts.summary: A Cross-tree narrative embedded in the Expanded Northern Passion in Harley 4196.

locators.value: ff. 76va–81r, within the Expanded Northern Passion at ff. 67r–86r

content_parts.label: IV. Finding of the Cross
content_parts.summary: A verse account for the feast of the Invention of the Cross from Harley 4196.

content_parts.label: VI. How þe Hali Cros Was Fundin be Seint Elaine
content_parts.summary: A northern verse Finding of the Cross text copied in Fairfax 14.

content_parts.label: VII. Exposition of the Holy Rood
content_parts.summary: A verse narrative for the feast of the Exaltation of the Cross from Harley 4196.

content_parts.label: VIII. Dispute Between Mary and the Cross — Vernon version
content_parts.summary: A stanzaic debate in which Mary and the Cross contest responsibility for Christ's suffering.

content_parts.label: IX. With an O and an I
content_parts.summary: A six-stanza Passion lyric with a recurring O-and-I refrain.

locators.value: ff. 90v–91r according to Morris's milestone mapping; DIMEV gives f. 90va

content_parts.label: X. With an O for an I
content_parts.summary: A short Passion lyric whose refrain pairs O and I.

content_parts.label: XI. The Invention of the Holy Cross
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose from the 20 May 1493 Golden Legend printing, edited by Morris as his item XI.

content_parts.label: XII. The Exaltation of the Cross
content_parts.summary: Middle English prose from the 20 May 1493 Golden Legend printing, edited by Morris as his item XII.

content_parts.label: XIII. The Symbols of the Passion — Royal version
content_parts.summary: A text-image Arma Christi sequence from Royal 17 A XXVII; the encoding preserves verse and captions but reduces the twenty-four miniature sequence to structural placeholders.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[4]/DIV2[13]/DIV3; 252 L, 29 LG, 26 P; printed pp. 170–196
ff. 72v–81r; O Vernicle ff. 72v–78v and thanksgiving prayer ff. 79r–80r, followed by further captions/text through f. 81r

content_parts.label: XIII. The Symbols of the Passion — Additional 22029 version
content_parts.summary: The Arma Christi roll version, whose twenty-four illustrations were supplied to Morris by Professor de la Motte; the encoding retains empty illustration placeholders rather than the pictures.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[4]/DIV2[14]/DIV3; 152 L, 24 LG, 24 empty P illustration placeholders; printed pp. 171–193

content_parts.label: Appendix
content_parts.summary: Container for three additional Middle English works from Royal 18 A X and Bodleian MS Liturg. 104; child work parts carry the additive item and line metrics.

content_parts.label: Dispute Between Mary and the Cross — Royal version
content_parts.summary: A longer Royal 18 A X version of the Mary-and-the-Cross debate, arranged against the corresponding stanzas of Morris's item VIII.

content_parts.label: Festivals of the Church
content_parts.summary: A verse sequence on church feasts, incomplete at the end in Royal 18 A X.
notes: The XML has 335 L elements. The British Library catalog describes the witness as incomplete at line 333; the difference likely reflects counting conventions and remains open.

content_parts.label: Horae de Cruce
content_parts.summary: English verse portions of the Hours of the Cross with the beginnings of preceding Latin prayers and rubrics, selected discontinuously from Bodleian MS Liturg. 104.
notes: DIMEV gives a broader locus of ff. 49–88, while the encoding's seven milestone sequences extend through f. 91r; manuscript autopsy is needed to reconcile the conventions.
Morris describes seventeen couplets, while the XML has 33 L elements rather than 34; the discrepancy is not silently corrected.

related_works.title: Legenda aurea sanctorum, sive Lombardica historia [English]: The Golden Legend
notes: Only the Invention and Exaltation prose passages from the 1493 printing are represented; the whole Golden Legend is not part of this cataloging subject.

open_questions.question: Which physical copy of the 20 May 1493 Golden Legend did Morris transcribe?
next_steps: Inspect Morris's papers or EETS production records and compare distinctive readings against extant copies.

open_questions.question: How should Morris's description of the 1493 printing as the third edition be reconciled with current catalogs that treat it as the second English edition or a reprint of 1483?
next_steps: Consult current Caxton and Wynkyn de Worde bibliographies; preserve both attributed descriptions meanwhile.

open_questions.question: Why do the XML milestones run through f. 91r while DIMEV gives ff. 49–88 for Horae de Cruce?
next_steps: Inspect all target leaves in MS Liturg. 104 or order images and reconcile modern and historical foliation.

open_questions.question: Does Morris's statement of seventeen couplets reflect a missing or merged line where the XML has 33 L elements?
next_steps: Collate printed pp. 222–224 against the manuscript surfaces and inspect line-container boundaries.

open_questions.question: Why does the British Library description refer to an incomplete ending at line 333 while the XML has 335 L elements?
next_steps: Inspect Royal 18 A X ff. 130v–134v and compare catchwords, headings, and Morris's lineation.

open_questions.question: Should the history-of-scholarship note retain Morris's late-thirteenth-century date alongside the current Bodleian first-quarter-fourteenth-century date?
next_steps: Retain both with attribution and use the current catalog date for present-day witness metadata.

notes: Roman numerals follow Morris. Numbers I and V are absent because those Old English texts are outside the encoded selection, not because the cataloging omitted two represented works.
The two item-II versions, two item-XIII versions, and two Mary-and-the-Cross versions are counted separately because the edition and encoding transmit distinct textual states.
Illustration placeholders in the Symbols items are structural representation facts; they are not prose paragraphs and do not make those items mixed-form.
Manuscript dates are first-known-attestation evidence and are not silently converted into composition dates.
